The recovered combat reconcile (designs/audit/2026-06-28-combat-critique/distillation_input_reconcile.md:137) explicitly argues fallacy-as-foul "is ALREADY present in social as rule-penalised moves." So the general ARGUMENTATIVE foul layer is thin, not absent. Leverage→medium: the Recall-foul gap is logged P2 sim debt (play-culture dependency, not a broken resolver), and the worst-defined sub-claim (Suspicion Token) is a one-line terminology reconcile, not a high-leverage structural hole.
verdict.refined_improvement: Two separable repairs, both no-new-resolver. (1) TERMINOLOGY (trivial, do first): delete the orphaned "Suspicion Token" in contest_extensions.md:47 Sanction row and replace its effect with "places a Doubt Marker on the obscurer (self-mark)" — folding it into the single canonical Doubt Marker per ED-897, which also closes the audit-workflow collision. (2) FOUL-DETECTION (the substantive fix): rather than a new Adjudicator Foul-Response table, reuse the EXISTING Appraise roll as the detection channel — a respondent who suspects a fabricated citation may, in place of their own Argue, spend the exchange on an Appraise vs the citer's Charisma÷2 (already the Appraise Ob at social_contest_v30.md:123); on net success the +2D Recall bonus is voided AND a Doubt Marker lands on the citer. Scope it to formal adjudicated proceedings only and gate on the adjudicator perceiving it, mirroring §9.4b's existing Scope + Visibility-gate pattern (lines 471-473) so it inherits the established structure instead of bolting on a parallel one. Leave the genre-dodge alone or address it minimally — the Doubt-Marker-on-Obscuring rule (line 170) already partially taxes the dodge in the Obscuring case, so a full anti-dodge mechanic risks over-penalizing legitimate CROSS play.

gaps.title: Parliament and 'Private Negotiation' resolve as a zero-sum vote-contest, not a supply-for-redress bargain — the mixed-motive surplus has no representation
gaps.research_basis: Negotiation (deliberation Part IV) is mixed-motive, not zero-sum: parties cooperate to create a surplus and compete over its division (Walton-McKersie integrative vs distributive; Nash bargain; ZOPA). Parliaments (politics model 8) are supply-for-redress bargains where each side can gain, not pure win/lose contests.
gaps.system_state: Every proceeding type — Formal/Grand Contest, BG Parliamentary Vote, Hybrid, and the 'Private Negotiation' type — resolves on a single 0-10 Persuasion Track with one winner. There is no axis on which both sides can simultaneously gain.

locations.quote: Scale: 0–10. Side A wins at ≥ 7. Side B wins at ≤ 3. Compromise zone: 4–6.
Persuasion Track ≥ 7 = motion passes; ≤ 3 = motion fails; 4–6 = referred to committee.
winner: Optional[str]         # 'A' / 'B' / None (compromise)
Negotiation compromise resolution (ZOPA-style) | Identified as structurally different from Persuasion Track, not designed

gaps.critique: The single track encodes a pure distributive (positional) contest: every unit Side A gains, Side B loses. A supply-for-redress parliament and a private negotiation are precisely the cases where the surplus is the point — Crown can get its tax IF it grants the redress the backbenchers want, and BOTH walk away ahead of no-deal. The spec acknowledges the gap exists ('structurally different from Persuasion Track') but defers it, leaving the system to model bargaining as a tug-of-war. Crucially this is NOT an 'add a new subsystem' problem: the engine already owns a working integrative primitive in npc_behavior §Step 3 (Offer), where incentives — advisory role, territory governance, Wealth commitment, Belief-alignment — each buy an Ob reduction and pay out in Disposition/Mandate/Wealth deltas. That primitive is stranded in Recruitment.
gaps.churn_relevance: A distributive-only resolver suppresses the richest churn source the research identifies: the trade. A ZOPA negotiation whose FAILURE modes (no-deal, a holdout demanding a side-payment, a deal that wins the table but blows the ratification) generate exactly the emergent-narrative churn the axiom prizes. Collapsing it to one track turns a generative bargain into a binary outcome.
gaps.improvement: Add a 'Negotiation' resolution mode (a parameterization of the existing contest, not a new engine) that runs alongside the Persuasion Track rather than replacing the kernel. Keep the sigma resolver for WHO has leverage (track position sets each side's BATNA / reservation point: Side A's reservation = the outcome they'd get if the contest resolved now). Then borrow the Recruitment Offer table wholesale as the integrative move: each exchange, a side may table an Offer that spends a faction-stat delta (Wealth -1/season, Mandate -1, a settlement-targeted Obligation, a Disposition concession) in exchange for moving the OTHER side's reservation point. ZOPA exists when the two reservation points overlap; a deal in the overlap pays each side its Nash split of the surplus as opposing-signed Disposition/Mandate deltas (reuse the §7.2.1 track-distance weighting — Track 4 = 60/40 — to set the split). No-deal falls back to the current zero-sum track. This reuses sigma-A, the Offer primitive, the flat Disposition axis, and the existing weighting table.

verdict.evidence_quote: winner: Optional[str]         # 'A' / 'B' / None (compromise)
verdict.reasoning:
Every cited quote is located verbatim and in-context. social_contest §2 Step 4 ("Scale: 0-10. Side A wins at >= 7. Side B wins at <= 3. Compromise zone: 4-6"), §10 BG Vote Resolution ("Persuasion Track >= 7 = motion passes; <= 3 = motion fails; 4-6 = referred to committee"), and §12 Future design work ("Negotiation compromise resolution (ZOPA-style) | Identified as structurally different from Persuasion Track, not designed") all match. contest.py confirms the single-winner shape: ContestResult.winner is Optional[str] 'A'/'B'/None, and track_movement is "Toward winner's position (signed)". The single track IS purely distributive: the same scalar that advances Side A retreats Side B, with no second axis on which both gain.

The critical "already-handled" check FAILS to refute the finding: I grepped contest_extensions.md, social_contest_v30_infill.md, and contest.py for negotiat|ZOPA|reservation|BATNA|integrative|surplus|mixed-motive|supply-for-redress — ZERO matches across all three. The gap is genuinely unfilled; the spec itself admits this in §12. Private Negotiation (§2 Step 5; §9.7 Niflhel) explicitly resolves on the same Persuasion Track or "exchange majority" with a single winner — no integrative axis exists for it either, confirming the finding's claim that the named "Private Negotiation" type is also distributive.

The finding's engine-discipline framing is also verified, which is what makes the improvement credible rather than a bolt-on. The integrative primitive it points to genuinely exists and is genuinely stranded: npc_behavior_v30 §9.5 Step 3 ("Offer") gives a table where each incentive (Advisory role -1, Territory governance -2, Wealth commitment -1, Belief-alignment -2, Hooks) buys an Ob reduction — confirmed verbatim at lines 1007-1019, and it lives only in the Recruitment procedure (PP-642). The distillation report independently corroborates the reuse mandate: "the social contest IS the combat kernel" / sigma-A (lines 67, 201), so layering a Negotiation mode as a parameterization of the existing sigma resolver (rather than a new engine) is the correct discipline.

Leverage stays high: a distributive-only resolver suppresses exactly the trade/no-deal/holdout/ratification-failure churn the world-churn axiom prizes, and parliaments/private negotiations are precisely the proceeding types where surplus is the point. It is not overstated — the system really does collapse a mixed-motive bargain to a tug-of-war for ALL proceeding types. The one tempering note is that this is a self-acknowledged deferred item (§12), not a hidden defect, and effort is genuinely high.
verdict.refined_improvement: Adopt the original Negotiation-mode proposal with one correction to keep it faithful to the reused primitive. The Recruitment Offer table moves an OB (difficulty), not a track position directly — so an Offer should not "move the other side's reservation point" as a raw track edit. Instead: (1) Keep the sigma resolver / Persuasion Track for WHO has leverage; each side's current track position sets its reservation/BATNA (the outcome it would get if the contest resolved now). (2) Borrow the §9.5 Step-3 Offer table wholesale as the integrative move: each exchange a side may table one Offer that spends a faction-stat delta (Wealth -1/season, Mandate -1, a settlement-targeted Obligation per §6.1, a Disposition concession) and, mirroring how the Offer table buys an Ob reduction in Recruitment, applies a -1/-2 modifier to the OTHER side's Argue Ob in the next exchange — i.e., the Offer literally lowers the price the counterparty must pay to accept, widening ZOPA through the existing Ob channel rather than a new track operation. (3) ZOPA exists when the two reservation positions overlap; a deal in the overlap pays each side its split as opposing-signed Disposition/Mandate deltas, using the §7.2.1 track-distance weighting (Track 4 = 60/40, Track 5 = 55/45, Track 6 = 50/50) to set the division. (4) No-deal falls back to the current zero-sum track resolution. This reuses sigma-A, the Offer Ob-modifier primitive (not a track bolt-on), the flat Disposition axis, the existing §7.2.1 weighting table, and the §6.1 Obligation machinery for settlement/Wager-style commitments — adding no new engine. Cap Offers at one per exchange per side (matching "Maximum one incentive per recruitment attempt") to prevent stacking, which §9.5 already flags as coercion rather than bargaining.

gaps.title: The coalition Lead is answerable to a shared dice pool, not a faction win-set — Putnam's two-level game is structurally absent
gaps.research_basis: Putnam's two-level game: a negotiator plays two boards at once — the table and domestic ratification — bound by a WIN-SET. A move that strengthens one table can lose the other; a deal outside the domestic win-set is repudiated even if the table loved it.
gaps.system_state: In a multi-party contest a side nominates one Lead per exchange; the coalition is mechanically just a shared Concentration pool summed from members' Focus/Spirit. Nothing the Lead concedes at the table can be repudiated by the coalition.

locations.quote: Each side nominates one Lead per exchange (may change between exchanges).
Coalition Concentration — shared pool (PP-237):** Concentration tracks on a shared pool equal to the sum of all coalition members' (3 × Focus) + (2 × Spirit) at contest setup.
The succession ratification is my leverage. I will not surrender it without Southernmost access terms.

gaps.critique: The coalition is a single aggregate body — members contribute dice and corroboration but have no separable interest the Lead must satisfy. So there is no second board: the Lead can accept any compromise the table reaches and the coalition is bound, with no ratification step. The damning detail is that the win-set ALREADY EXISTS in the fiction — Vaynard's Belief 3 is a textbook domestic constraint ('I will not surrender my vote without [a specific concession]') — and the engine has the primitive to represent it (Convictions/Beliefs per conviction_taxonomy + npc_behavior). The two-level structure is written into the story but has no mechanical home, so the GM must adjudicate ratification by hand.
gaps.churn_relevance: The two-level game is a churn ENGINE: the antechamber maneuver, the lead who oversells at the table and gets repudiated at home, the holdout backbencher whose private win-set blocks the public deal — these are emergent-narrative gold, and the research says they are the point of modeling negotiation as politics. A shared pool erases all of it.
gaps.improvement: Give each coalition member a WIN-SET expressed in the existing Belief/Disposition primitives: a member's win-set = the set of contest outcomes that do not violate an active Belief or push their Disposition-toward-the-deal below a threshold. At resolution, run a cheap ratification check (reuse the sigma resolver, bare-stat Instance-B form): if the Lead accepts a compromise OUTSIDE a member's win-set, that member rolls to ratify or defects (Disposition -, withdraws their share of the shared pool retroactively, or triggers a Succession/Chain Contest). Concretely: a member whose active Belief is contradicted by the deal applies the npc_behavior defection path (Disposition drops, returns to prior faction). This bolts no new subsystem on — it wires Beliefs + Disposition + the existing defection rule into §9.2 as the second board.

verdict.evidence_quote: His Belief 3: "The succession ratification is my leverage. I will not surrender it without Southernmost access terms." Consequence: players must either give him Southernmost access OR find another way to ratify succession without his vote
verdict.reasoning: The gap is real and unfilled. §9.2 (social_contest_v30.md:449-450, mirrored in params/contest.md §177 PP-237) models a coalition as ONLY a summed Concentration pool plus one rotating Lead and optional Corroborate — Composure/Rattled are individual but there is no separable member interest the Lead must satisfy, and no ratification of the deal the Lead strikes. I grepped designs/, designs/architecture/, params/, and sim/personal/contest.py for win-set / two-level / Putnam / ratify-the-deal / repudiate-the-deal: ZERO mechanical matches. The "ratification" hits in the corpus are all narrative (Torben's succession in arcs_28_30) or treaty/document ratification (faction_layer), not a coalition member repudiating a table outcome. The contest.py sim has no coalition-member-interest object at all. The damning detail checks out verbatim: narrative_scenario_chains.md:690-691 states Vaynard's Belief 3 as a textbook domestic win-set ("I will not surrender [my leverage] without [a specific concession]") and line 692-693 makes it an explicit GM-adjudicated branch ("players must either give him Southernmost access OR find another way to ratify succession without his vote") — i.e. the win-set is hand-adjudicated, with no mechanical home. IMPORTANT nuance the critique slightly overstates: the npc_behavior §9 "defection" rule (lines 1043-1048: Spirit pool, TN 7, Ob = current Disposition toward recruiting faction → returns to prior faction) is a RECRUITMENT-loyalty check, not a coalition-member-repudiates-table-deal check; its trigger is recruitment Belief-contradiction, not "Lead conceded outside my win-set." So the primitive the improvement leans on EXISTS but is wired to a different event — it is genuinely not connected to §9.2. Engine-discipline check passes: the distillation report (distillation_coherence_report.md:55, 67, 201) confirms the contest exchange IS sigma Instance A and there is a bare-stat Instance B form, so a cheap ratification roll reuses the existing kernel rather than bolting on a subsystem. Leverage stays high: this is a multiplicative churn source (antechamber maneuver, oversell-then-repudiate, holdout backbencher) sitting on top of primitives the engine already has (Beliefs, Disposition ±5 per knots_v30, Obligations §6.1, the sigma kernel).
verdict.refined_improvement: Wire the second board into §9.2 by reusing three EXISTING primitives, adding no subsystem: (1) Each coalition member carries a win-set = the set of contest outcomes that does not contradict an active Belief (conviction_taxonomy/npc_behavior Belief structure) and does not push their Disposition-toward-the-deal below a threshold (knots_v30 ±5 scale, e.g. -3, matching the existing recruitment-defection Disposition floor). (2) At resolution, before the §6.1 Obligation is bound, run a ratification check on each member whose win-set the accepted compromise violates: reuse the sigma resolver in bare-stat Instance-B form (per distillation_coherence_report Instance B, P=Φ((μ−Ob)/σ)) — Spirit-based, Ob keyed to that member's Disposition toward the deal, exactly paralleling the npc_behavior §9 Belief integrity check (line 1046) rather than inventing a new roll. (3) On failed ratification, fire the existing consequence menu, NOT a new one: Disposition drop and return-to-prior-faction (the §9 defection path), OR — because a coalition is a contest, not a recruitment — withdraw that member's share of the shared Concentration pool retroactively and convert the unresolved tension into a Chain Contest (§6.3) or Succession Contest (§7.2). Tighten one thing the original got loose: do NOT route this through the recruitment-defection rule wholesale (different trigger/Ob); instead state §9.2 ratification as its own one-line clause that *cites* the Belief-integrity roll form and the §6.1 binding step. This keeps the binding where contest outcomes already become enforceable (Obligations) and gives Vaynard's Belief 3 a mechanical home so the GM no longer adjudicates it by hand.

gaps.title: No Priest-Klein selection gate: lopsided disputes are routed away from full contest only by GM fiat
gaps.research_basis: Litigation (deliberation III.E, Priest-Klein): cases SELECT for trial precisely when they are closest; lopsided disputes settle out. A well-configured dispute system should mechanically route one-sided cases AWAY from full contest, reserving the expensive resolver for genuinely contested ones.
gaps.system_state: The only selection mechanism is a GM advisory; once a contest is called, even a hopeless case runs the full exchange structure (with a few asymmetric concessions like halved resistance).

locations.quote: GMs should not call for a contest when one side has no plausible case
Disadvantaged party (accused, petitioner) faces halved resistance (round up) when moving the Persuasion Track.
Persuasion Track starts at **7** (Church near-decisive before Exchange 1 — institutional fait accompli)

gaps.critique: Asymmetric proceedings handle institutional TILT (biased starting track, halved resistance) but that is not selection — it still runs the full contest, just with the thumb on the scale. There is no pre-contest test of 'is this dispute close enough to be worth contesting?' that diverts a lopsided case to a cheaper resolver. The Excommunication design even half-recognizes this ('The correct strategic counter is preventing the filing, not defending at Tribunal') — i.e. a one-sided case should never reach contest — but the diversion is narrated, not mechanized.
gaps.churn_relevance: Priest-Klein selection is itself a churn router: it doesn't suppress story, it relocates it. A lopsided dispute that can't be fully contested becomes a settlement, a side-payment, or a holdout — generative outcomes — instead of a foregone-conclusion contest that burns table time for no uncertainty. This sharpens the churn density of the contests that DO run.
gaps.improvement: Add a one-roll pre-contest 'lopsidedness gate' using the bare-stat sigma (Instance B): compare the two sides' expected argue-pool advantage at setup (a quantity the GM already estimates for resistance). If the gap exceeds a threshold (e.g., one side's effective leverage > ~2x), the contest does not open — instead the stronger side dictates terms and the weaker side gets a single Offer/Concession move (reuse FG-1's Offer table) to extract a side-payment or a settlement-targeted Obligation in lieu of contesting. Close cases (gap within the band) proceed to full contest. This reuses the resistance estimate the GM already computes and the Offer primitive; it adds one comparison, not a subsystem.

verdict.reasoning: All cited quotes verified. The §1 advisory "GMs should not call for a contest when one side has no plausible case" lives verbatim in social_contest_v30_infill.md:20 (the finding correctly attributes it to §1/§2 setup). The §7 halved-resistance quote (social_contest_v30.md:320) and the §7.1 Excommunication quotes — "Persuasion Track starts at 7" (line 530) and "The correct strategic counter is preventing the filing, not defending at Tribunal" (line 535) — are all present. I grepped the full designs/ tree for any closeness-based selection/routing primitive (lopsided|one-sided|hopeless|settle out|Priest-Klein|selection gate|triage|filing). The ONLY merits-closeness mechanism is the GM advisory — confirmed narrative, not mechanized. §7 asymmetric proceedings and §7.1 Excommunication handle institutional TILT (biased start, halved resistance) but still run the full exchange structure, exactly as the critique says. The richest "don't run it" machinery in the corpus — Parliamentary Stay (§10.1) and Heresy Investigation closure conditions (§7.3.2, 8 conditions: Inquisitor death/demotion, target defection, CI<55 Stay) — routes on WHO HAS INSTITUTIONAL STANDING and procedural posture, NOT on how lopsided the merits are. So those do not fill the gap; the finding survives the most-important already-handled check. The improvement's engine vocabulary is sound: the distillation report (lines 55-56, 69) confirms sigma Instance B (bare-stat, legible odds, the "no-pool limit" where a mu-shift of +delta and Ob-shift of -delta are identical) is a real named kernel that domain_actions already use, and that the contest exchange IS sigma-A — so a one-roll setup leverage comparison reuses the existing kernel rather than bolting on a subsystem. One real defect in the improvement: it depends on "FG-1's Offer table," but no Offer/Concession primitive exists in the contest spec — the only "concede" is the in-exchange Concede-a-Point forfeit (§4 Step 5), which is mid-contest, not a pre-contest settlement move. The settlement payoff must instead reuse the Obligation primitive (§6.1), which already supports settlement-targeted commitments, to stay reuse-only.
verdict.evidence_quote: A contest is initiated when: (a) two or more parties with opposed positions are present, AND (b) the outcome is uncertain and consequential. GMs should not call for a contest when one side has no plausible case or when the outcome is predetermined by prior Domain Actions.
verdict.refined_improvement: Add a one-roll pre-contest "lopsidedness gate" parameterized as sigma Instance B (the bare-stat, legible-odds kernel confirmed in the distillation report; domain_actions already run on it — no new subsystem). At setup the GM already estimates audience resistance from faction Stability (§2 Step 4); extend that same estimate to compare the two sides' effective argue-pool leverage. If one side's effective leverage exceeds the other's by more than a threshold band (e.g. >~2x), the contest does not open: the stronger side dictates terms and the weaker side gets a single move to extract a concession in lieu of contesting. Crucially, do NOT route the payoff through an "Offer table" (no such primitive exists in the corpus, and the referenced FG-1 artifact is unverified). Reuse the EXISTING Obligation primitive (§6.1), including settlement-targeted Obligations — the lopsided dispute resolves as the stronger side imposing one verifiable Obligation, or accepting a settlement-targeted Obligation from the weaker side, in lieu of running exchanges. Close cases (leverage gap inside the band) proceed to the full exchange structure. This adds one Instance-B comparison at setup plus one Obligation write — both already in the engine.

strengths.title: The Wager Obligation is a correctly-built mixed-motive (game-theoretic) instrument — proof the engine CAN hold more than the agonistic sense
strengths.research_basis: Part 0: the game-theoretic sense is strategic interdependence, often mixed-motive / positive-sum, where present cooperation is extended against future delivery. Part D.4: 'strike a bargain' as a distinct thing a procedure does.
strengths.system_state: The Wager Obligation lets a contest winner extend present trust against a future, verifiable condition the loser commits to meet, with structured success/fail/partial resolution and detailed edge cases.

locations.quote: Obligations may take a future-conditional form when the winning side explicitly extends present trust against future delivery.
Condition met within timeframe: Obligation discharged successfully. Counterparty grants the negotiated benefit

strengths.critique: This is the one place the contest genuinely transcends zero-sum: the winner forgoes an immediate decisive outcome in exchange for a future positive-sum state (both parties prefer the cooperative path), and the loser's commitment is achievable and verifiable. It maps cleanly onto the game-theoretic / trust-extension sense and is the canonical Warden-Conviction cooperation mechanism (Edeyja Arc E). It demonstrates the engine is NOT intrinsically agonistic-only — which is exactly why the gaps in FG-1/FG-3 are under-configurations rather than impossibilities.
strengths.churn_relevance: The Wager is high churn done right: its FAILURE MODES (counterparty death, faction collapse, structural impossibility — §6.1.1) each fork the narrative, and the future-conditional clock is a live story seed for seasons. This is the template the Compromise-bargain (FG-1) and Inquiry (FG-3) branches should imitate.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited quotes are verbatim-accurate in designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md §6.1 (lines 255 and 258). The Wager Obligation is genuinely a mixed-motive / trust-extension instrument: §6.1 line 255 states the winner "explicitly extends present trust against future delivery," with content that must be verifiable, achievable, and time-bound (lines 255 a/b/c) — exactly the game-theoretic positive-sum framing the research_basis invokes. The structured success/fail/partial table (lines 258-260) and the §6.1.1 edge-case table (lines 264-278, ED-778) exist as described, and each edge case (counterparty death → discharge neutrally + Renown; institutional collapse → suspend/resume; structural impossibility → fail-forward + Conviction Scar; plus two PC-death transfer cases) is a genuine narrative fork, validating the churn_relevance claim.

The load-bearing comparative claim — that the Wager is the ONE place the contest transcends zero-sum, making FG-1/FG-3 under-configurations rather than impossibilities — survives an adversarial check of the obvious counter-candidate, the Compromise outcome. Compromise is explicitly NOT positive-sum: §6.3 line 298 says "the tension is deferred, not resolved," and line 311 resolves a 3-chain stalemate as "a cold equilibrium" with frozen Disposition. So the contest's non-Decisive band is a deferred/negative-sum stalemate, not a cooperative surplus — the Wager is indeed the sole mixed-motive surplus mechanism. The canonical anchor also resolves: npc_behavior_v30 §1.2 is the Conviction Taxonomy (line 24), and the Edeyja arc map (lines 630+) confirms cooperative arcs ("Arc B: The Collaboration," line 637; Arc E "The Wager" referenced from contest spec). params/contest.md, by contrast, has NO Wager/positive-sum content (grep returned no matches), meaning this transcendence lives entirely in the prose spec, not the formula layer — consistent with the finding treating it as a structural/design strength rather than a math primitive.

One honest correction on leverage: as a pure strength its standalone actionability is low; its real value is evidential (existence proof). "medium" is defensible only because of that scaffolding role for FG-1/FG-3, not because the Wager itself needs work — so I'd hold it at medium with that caveat rather than raise it.
verdict.evidence_quote: When a contest ends in Compromise (Persuasion Track 4–6), the tension is deferred, not resolved.
verdict.refined_improvement: No improvement needed for the Wager itself (it is correctly built). The finding's actionable payload is its consequence for FG-1/FG-3: when those branches are configured, do NOT bolt on a new bargaining subsystem — instead instantiate them as Wager-shaped future-conditional Obligations reusing the existing §6.1 / §6.1.1 machinery (clock_registry tracking, verifiable/achievable/time-bound condition, the three-way met/not-met/partial resolution, and the same edge-case fork table). I.e., generalize the Wager's verifiable-future-condition contract beyond its current Projection-genre + Consequence-Resonant-Style + Warden-Conviction gating (§6.1 line 255/262) rather than authoring parallel Compromise-bargain or Inquiry resolvers. This keeps the positive-sum capability on the existing sigma-resolver/Obligation-clock rails, honoring no-bolt-on engine discipline.

strengths.title: Obligations are a real commitment store - but post-contest output, not in-contest substrate
strengths.research_basis: Commitment in deliberation has two timescales: the in-dialogue store (what you've conceded mid-argument) and the durable upshot (what you are now bound to having lost). Valoria nails the second and is silent on the first.
strengths.system_state: A decisive win produces a binding Obligation - a verifiable, time-bound commitment tracked as a clock that constrains the loser's behavior across seasons, including blocking NPC priority-tree actions that would violate it.

locations.quote: A Decisive win (Persuasion Track ≥ 7 or ≤ 3) in a Formal or Grand Contest produces a binding **Obligation** — a mechanical commitment that persists across seasons.
the NPC priority tree is modified: any action that would violate the Obligation is blocked for the Obligation's duration unless the faction enters Survival priority
Obligations are tracked as clocks per clock_registry_v30.

strengths.critique: Strength to credit honestly: Obligations are exactly the durable end of the commitment structure done right - verifiable, achievable, clock-tracked, behaviorally binding, with rich failure-mode churn (Wager edge cases, violation consequences). This proves the project CAN build a commitment ledger on its existing clock primitive. The gap is purely one of timescale and granularity: Obligation is the macro upshot of a whole contest, written only at the decisive moment, whereas the research's store is the per-move substrate FROM WHICH that upshot should emerge. The same clock machinery that hosts Obligations is the obvious substrate for the in-contest store (FG-1).
strengths.churn_relevance: Obligations are textbook churn (a won contest constrains an NPC faction for seasons, and its failure modes spawn arcs). This finding is the constructive bridge: it shows the in-contest store is buildable with the very primitive that already makes Obligations work, so FG-1 is not a new subsystem - it is Obligations-grade ledgering moved earlier and made finer-grained.
strengths.improvement: Reuse the clock_registry Obligation pattern at exchange granularity for the in-contest store; on Decisive win, fold the store's strongest conceded/unanswered entries into the named Obligation so the macro commitment literally emerges from the accumulated micro-commitments (bottom-up with top-down validation by the adjudicator/track).

verdict.reasoning: All three cited quotes are verbatim-accurate in designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md §6.1 (lines 242, 286, 282). The strength is real and correctly characterized: Obligations are a genuine durable commitment store — verifiable, achievable, clock-tracked (clock_registry_v30, which exists as a canonical primitive at designs/provincial/clock_registry_v30.md), behaviorally binding (modifies the NPC priority tree), with rich failure-mode churn (Wager edge cases §6.1.1, violation consequences, generational transfer). The claimed gap — that the system nails the DURABLE upshot but is silent on the IN-CONTEST per-move store — also holds under independent inspection. Grepping the full spec for in-contest persistent state surfaces only: the Persuasion Track (a single scalar position), a Doubt Marker ("Only one Doubt Marker active at a time. New replaces old," consumed on use — ephemeral, not a ledger), Concentration/Composure (depletion meters), and the "hidden GM ledger" (§2 Step 7 / §4 Step 7 — a record of rolls, NOT a mechanically-binding store of conceded/unanswered points). The "Concede a Point" forfeit action (line 187) exists but only yields +1D and a track tick; it does not record the conceded content as a binding entry. So nothing in the contest spec models "what you've conceded mid-argument" as a substrate. The contest.py sim models no Obligation or in-contest store either, consistent with Obligations living at the post-contest narrative layer. The finding is constructive and accurate.
verdict.evidence_quote: A Decisive win (Persuasion Track ≥ 7 or ≤ 3) in a Formal or Grand Contest produces a binding **Obligation** — a mechanical commitment that persists across seasons.
verdict.refined_improvement: Sound as written but tighten to respect engine discipline. Do NOT instantiate a clock per conceded point (that would multiply clock_registry entries and hit the ED-619 3-Obligation tractability concern at exchange granularity). Instead, reuse the EXISTING hidden GM ledger (§4 Step 7) — which already records each exchange — as the in-contest store, by adding two typed fields per exchange entry: conceded (the position the loser yielded) and unanswered (a CROSS/Obscuring argument the opponent never rebutted). These are pure ledger annotations, no new state machine and no per-move clock. Then on Decisive win, the §6.1 Obligation-naming step reads the ledger's strongest conceded/unanswered entries as the candidate set from which the winning side names the binding Obligation — so the macro Obligation literally emerges bottom-up from the accumulated micro-commitments, with top-down validation by the adjudicator/track threshold already in §6.1. A clock is minted only at the Decisive moment (one per contest, as today); the in-contest store stays as cheap ledger metadata. This is FG-1 implemented as ledger-grade annotation rather than a new subsystem, keeping the single durable artifact on the clock primitive.

strengths.title: Appraise-first exchanges model the opponent's state - the read half of a dialogue, kept honest
strengths.research_basis: Dutilh Novaes's internalized Prover-Skeptic and Walton-Krabbe's dialogue types both require each party to MODEL the other's commitments and reactions; deliberation is not blind dice but reading what the other side is committed to and exploiting it.
strengths.system_state: Every exchange opens with an Appraise roll that senses the audience's current boost, and at high degree reveals a specific Belief, resistance threshold, or the opponent's emotional state; NPC Beliefs are a persistent propositional structure revealed and revised through contest play.

locations.quote: Each exchange's Appraise senses the CURRENT state of the audience (which may have shifted from Doubt Markers, track movement, or strain). This is not a re-attempt of the same question — it is a fresh perception of changed circumstances.

Beliefs are revealed to players through:
- Contest Appraise step (Overwhelming: one Belief revealed per §4 social_contest_v30.md)

strengths.critique: Credit where due: the Appraise-first structure (load-bearing per the distillation report) is the correct read primitive - it makes each exchange a fresh model of the opponent/audience, which is the perceptual half of a commitment-store dialogue. And NPC Beliefs ARE persistent propositional state. But the asymmetry is the tell: the system can read and revise the NPC's standing commitments (Beliefs) yet maintains no symmetric ledger of the propositions exchanged IN the contest by either side. The read half exists; the recorded-commitment half it should be reading does not. Appraise reveals a pre-existing Belief, never 'what your opponent has conceded this contest.'
strengths.churn_relevance: Appraise is good churn (each read is fresh perception of changed state). The constructive point: once an in-contest store exists (FG-1), Appraise has a far richer target - it could reveal an opponent's open doubts or vulnerable concessions, not just a static Belief - turning the existing read primitive into the engine that surfaces commitment-store contradictions.
strengths.improvement: Extend the existing Appraise degree table with one row: Overwhelming additionally reveals one entry in the opponent's in-contest commitment store (a concession they made or an unanswered doubt against them), reusing the current reveal mechanism - no new roll, just a new payload for the read that already happens.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited quotes verify verbatim. social_contest_v30.md §4 Step 1 (line 125) reads exactly: "Each exchange's Appraise senses the CURRENT state of the audience (which may have shifted from Doubt Markers, track movement, or strain). This is not a re-attempt of the same question — it is a fresh perception of changed circumstances." And npc_behavior_v30.md §3.1 (line 386-387): "Beliefs are revealed to players through: - Contest Appraise step (Overwhelming: one Belief revealed per §4 social_contest_v30.md)." So the strength claim is true: Appraise is a per-exchange read of changed state, and NPC Beliefs are persistent propositional structure revealed/revised through play (§3.1/§3.2). The distillation report independently confirms the Appraise-first contest is load-bearing ("Load-bearing, DO NOT cut ... the Appraise-first contest") and that the contest IS the sigma read/resolve kernel.

The asymmetry the critique names is real. I grepped the full corpus (designs/scene, params/contest.md, params/contest_extensions.md, sim/personal/contest.py) for any in-contest record of propositions exchanged. There is none. What exists adjacent to the claimed gap and does NOT fill it: (1) the Doubt Marker (§4 Step 4 / params line 95) is purely a numeric margin penalty — "−2 to opponent's next winning margin; one active at a time; consumed on use" — not a readable proposition; the contest.py sim carries no commitment/concession/store structure, only Beliefs. (2) "Concede a Point" (§4 Step 5, line 187) is a forfeit action granting +1D, not a recorded ledger entry the opponent can later Appraise. So the read half exists; the symmetric in-contest recorded-commitment half it could be reading does not. The critique's "Appraise reveals a pre-existing Belief, never what your opponent has conceded this contest" is accurate.

One scoping note that tempers (not refutes) leverage: the improvement is contingent on FG-1 (an in-contest commitment store) existing first — the finding itself says so. Without FG-1 there is no store for Appraise to read. So FG-5's value is conditional/downstream, which is why medium (not high) is the honest read.
verdict.evidence_quote:
| Appraise net | Resonant Style info |
|---|---|
| Overwhelming (3+) | Primary Resonant Style revealed (in addition to one Belief per existing rules) |
| Overwhelming (4+) | Both primary and secondary Resonant Style revealed |
verdict.refined_improvement: Endorse the original, with the engine-discipline framing made explicit so it lands as a payload reuse, not a new subsystem. params/contest_extensions.md ALREADY demonstrates the exact pattern: it extends the Appraise Overwhelming degree row with a richer reveal payload (Resonant Style at 3+, secondary at 4+) "in addition to one Belief per existing rules." The FG-5 improvement is the same move: once FG-1's in-contest commitment store exists, add one further Overwhelming payload line — Appraise additionally surfaces one entry from the opponent's in-contest store (a concession they made, or an unanswered doubt standing against them), reusing the existing reveal mechanism and the existing Appraise roll. No new roll, no new resolver, no new bucket — it rides the sigma read that already happens and reads a store FG-1 already maintains. Strictly gate on FG-1 landing first (the finding already states this dependency); absent FG-1 there is nothing to reveal.

strengths.title: The agon itself is well-built: a bidirectional, asymmetric merit contest whose failure modes are churn, not bugs
strengths.research_basis: Caillois AGON = regulated contest of merit under equalized conditions, where the outcome is earned and legible. The politics companion treats a deliberative game's agon as the baseline against which other families are added; a strong agon is a precondition, not a defect.
strengths.system_state: The contest resolves via a single will+advantage kernel (Argue pool vs TN, margin vs resistance -> Persuasion Track movement), with explicit asymmetric handicaps and a compromise zone that spawns follow-up scenes rather than terminating.

locations.quote: Scale: 0–10. Side A wins at ≥ 7. Side B wins at ≤ 3. Compromise zone: 4–6.
When a contest ends in Compromise (Persuasion Track 4–6), the tension is deferred, not resolved. The unresolved contest generates a Scene Slate entry for the following season
the social contest *is* the combat kernel

strengths.critique: This is the family the system gets right. The bidirectional track (either threshold winnable), the asymmetric resistance halving for the disadvantaged party, and the Compromise->Chain->Deadlock terminator mean even a stalemate produces narrative. Caillois's agon demands equalized conditions and a legible earned outcome; the contest delivers both. The Obligation mechanism (§6.1) turns the agon's verdict into a persistent world-state clock — the contest writes durable churn, not just a stat bump. Credit where due: as an agon, this is mature.
strengths.churn_relevance: Directly satisfies the churn axiom: the procedure's FAILURE MODE (Compromise) is not inert — it seeds a guaranteed follow-up scene and carries Disposition/Scar state forward. That is the 'failure generates story' pattern the philosophy names as high-value.

verdict.reasoning:
All three cited quotes verify against the working tree. (1) "Scale: 0–10. Side A wins at ≥ 7. Side B wins at ≤ 3. Compromise zone: 4–6." is exact at social_contest_v30.md §2 Step 4 (line 73) and restated in params/contest.md §Persuasion Track (line 100). (2) The Chain Contest compromise-deferral quote is exact at §6.3 (line 298). (3) The distillation-report quote is mildly mis-located: the finding attributes "the social contest *is* the combat kernel" to §1, but that exact phrase actually sits in §2 line 67 ("the social contest *is* the combat kernel — the card cites the explicit pool-construction parallel"); §1's line 41 frames the sigma resolver as "the contest engine." The attribution is off by one section but the substance is faithful, so this does not refute the finding.

The strength claim is corroborated beyond the cited lines, not overstated. The bidirectional track (either threshold winnable, §6 line 218), the asymmetric resistance-halving for the disadvantaged party (§7 lines 320 / §2 Step 5 "Halved for petitioner/accused"), and the Compromise→Chain→Deadlock terminator (§6.3 lines 300-311, with hard caps: third zero-movement → Compromise, max-chain-3 → cold equilibrium) all exist as described. The Obligation mechanism (§6.1) genuinely writes durable world-state: Obligations are tracked as clocks per clock_registry_v30 (line 282), NPC Obligations modify the NPC priority tree by blocking violating actions for the duration (line 286), and player Obligations carry Mandate/Reputation/Disposition consequences. The "failure generates story" pattern is real: Compromise is not inert — it seeds a guaranteed Scene Slate entry (player_agency §4.2 Priority 1) and carries Disposition + Conviction-Scar state forward (§6.3 lines 303-304). The contest also reaches the engine's second primitive: Resonant Style targeting (contest_extensions, npc_behavior_v30 §1.3) lets a winning argument produce persistent Conviction Scars (§6.2), which is exactly the armature-dot-product interpretation step. So the agon does what the finding credits it with, and the distillation report independently confirms it is the sigma kernel.

Leverage is the one place to correct. The finding is a STRENGTH with improvement="" and effort="low" — it proposes no change. A pure "credit where due" observation with no actionable lever cannot honestly be "high leverage"; leverage measures the value of acting, and there is nothing to act on. I down-rate to low. (One latent action does exist nearby — SIM-DEBT-04 / formally verifying social_contest on Sigma Instance A is OPEN per params/contest.md line 168 and called out in the distillation report §2 line 67 — but that is a verification task, not part of this strength finding.)
verdict.evidence_quote: the social contest *is* the combat kernel — the card cites the explicit pool-construction parallel: Composure=Cha×3 ∥ Vitality, strain ∥ damage, Focus defence ∥ Armour — but it's never been verified against σ
verdict.refined_improvement: As a strength finding it correctly proposes no design change — the agon is mature and should not be touched. The only adjacent actionable item is not a defect in the agon but a verification gap the finding could note: SIM-DEBT-04 is OPEN (params/contest.md line 168) and the distillation report flags that social_contest has "never been formally verified against σ" (Instance A). Recommendation: keep this as a no-op strength, but reference SIM-DEBT-04 so the credit ("this is the sigma kernel done right") is backed by an actual sim verification rather than asserted. No bolt-on subsystem; this reuses the existing sigma resolver and only asks for the deferred calibration pass.

strengths.title: Formal/Grand Contest is a clean Type-1 'the contest IS the deciding' engine, correctly bottom-up
strengths.research_basis: Politics companion D.1 Type 1: the competition sits AT the decision (council/assembly/adjudication); the contest itself produces the binding outcome.
strengths.system_state: The Formal and Grand Contest run per-exchange sigma rolls that accumulate on a Persuasion Track whose final position emerges into a binding Obligation; macro outcome is not hard-coded but emerges from granular moves with a top-down adjudicator/track constraint.

locations.quote: A Decisive win (Persuasion Track ≥ 7 or ≤ 3) in a Formal or Grand Contest produces a binding **Obligation** — a mechanical commitment that persists across seasons. The Obligation is the contest's lasting consequence in the game world, not just a stat change.
the social contest *is* the combat kernel

strengths.critique: This is the correct configuration for a Type-1 deliberative game: the deciding is performed move-by-move and the binding result (the Obligation clock) is an emergent function of the substrate, validated top-down by the track threshold. It maps exactly onto the philosophy's bottom-up-emergent / top-down-validation standard, and the Obligation-as-clock means a Type-1 win seeds future world-state rather than terminating in a stat bump.
strengths.churn_relevance: High churn done right: the Obligation persists across seasons and (for NPCs) edits the priority tree, so a Type-1 outcome generates ongoing decision pressure rather than closing the loop. This is the model the Type-3 gaps below should imitate.

verdict.evidence_quote: A Decisive win (Persuasion Track ≥ 7 or ≤ 3) in a Formal or Grand Contest produces a binding **Obligation** — a mechanical commitment that persists across seasons. The Obligation is the contest's lasting consequence in the game world, not just a stat change.
verdict.reasoning: Every load-bearing claim checks out against the corpus. (1) Both cited quotes are verbatim: §6.1 at social_contest_v30.md:242, and "the social contest *is* the combat kernel" at distillation_coherence_report.md:67 (the finding's "combat kernel" paraphrase is faithful). (2) The bottom-up-emergent mechanism is real in the sim: sim/personal/contest.py resolve_exchange() moves the track by (margin − resistance) per exchange (line 167), and run_contest accumulates those into final_persuasion_track with the ≥7/≤3 threshold as the top-down validation gate (lines 200-207, 248) — so the macro outcome genuinely emerges from granular moves rather than being hard-coded. This is exactly the philosophy's bottom-up-emergent / top-down-validation pattern. (3) The churn claim is independently confirmed and is the strongest part: social_contest_v30.md:286 — "the NPC priority tree is modified: any action that would violate the Obligation is blocked for the Obligation's duration unless the faction enters Survival priority" — so a Type-1 win edits the NPC priority tree (npc_behavior_v30.md §8 trees are real, e.g. §8.2-§8.10) and seeds ongoing decision pressure rather than terminating in a stat bump. (4) The research_basis "Type 1" framing is internally grounded, not fabricated: it originates in the critique workflow's own research prompt (.claude/wf_social_contest_critique.js:123-125, "TYPE 1 (the contest IS the deciding: council/assembly/combat/adjudication)"). One honest caveat that does NOT undercut the strength: the Obligation is a doc/clock-level construct (tracked per clock_registry_v30, social_contest_v30.md:282) — contest.py's ContestResult does not yet instantiate an Obligation object, and SIM-DEBT-04 (formally verifying social_contest on the Instance-A sigma kernel) is still open per distillation_coherence_report.md:67. That is a verification debt, not a design flaw, so leverage stays high.
verdict.refined_improvement: No design change needed — this is a correctly-configured strength and the model the Type-3 gaps should imitate. The only follow-up is verification, not a new subsystem: close SIM-DEBT-04 by formally verifying the social-contest exchange on the Instance-A sigma kernel (P = Φ((μ−Ob)/σ)) per the distillation report, and have sim/personal/contest.py's ContestResult emit the Obligation as a clock_registry_v30 entry on Decisive/Total outcomes so the track→Obligation→priority-tree-edit chain is exercised end-to-end in sim, not just specified in the doc. No bolt-on subsystem — reuse the existing sigma resolver and the clock primitive.

strengths.title: Succession Contest is a genuine Type-2 with an emergent faction-split that is exemplary pathology-as-churn
strengths.research_basis: Politics companion D.1 Type 2: the competition selects WHO decides (election/succession). The research prizes outcomes where the procedure's own logic seeds the next arc.
strengths.system_state: §7.2 reframes Grand Contest infrastructure as a leadership-selection procedure; a Compromise outcome does not terminate but spawns a track-distance-weighted faction split into a new schismatic sub-faction.

locations.quote: Compromise (4-6) | **Faction split**: factional sub-divisions emerge (e.g., losing claimant retains personal retainers, may be granted a province as semi-autonomous fief). New sub-faction acquires partial inheritance of original faction's stat values
Identity: New sub-faction acquires identity per losing claimant's Conviction (e.g., Faith → schismatic Church; Order → loyalist Crown remnant; Autonomy → independent Jarldom).

strengths.critique: This is the one place the system treats a non-decisive procedural outcome as GENERATIVE: a hung succession does not resolve, it BIFURCATES the political map and mints a new actor whose identity is read off the loser's Conviction via the armature. This is precisely the research's 'the failure mode seeds the next arc.' It proves the engine CAN do pathology-as-churn — which makes its absence everywhere else (FG-4) a configuration choice, not a capability gap.
strengths.churn_relevance: Maximal: the indecisive procedure manufactures a brand-new faction and a civil-war seam (with a Stability-3 floor explicitly tuned to keep it playable, not catastrophic). This is the template to generalize.

verdict.evidence_quote: Identity: New sub-faction acquires identity per losing claimant's Conviction (e.g., Faith → schismatic Church; Order → loyalist Crown remnant; Autonomy → independent Jarldom).
verdict.reasoning: Both cited quotes are verbatim accurate in designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md §7.2 (line 341) and §7.2.1 (line 359). The mechanic is real and exactly as characterized: a Compromise (Track 4-6) Succession outcome does not terminate — it bifurcates the faction, mints a new sub-faction with track-distance-weighted stat inheritance (60/40, 55/45, 50/50 per ED-762, line 351), a Stability-3 floor explicitly tuned to keep the civil-war seam playable not catastrophic (line 353), and reads the new actor's identity off the LOSER's Conviction. That Conviction-read is the genuine armature primitive, not a bolt-on: 'Faith' and 'Order' are canonical Convictions (#1 and #3 in conviction_taxonomy_v30.md lines 29/31), and the distillation_coherence_report (lines 41, 207) confirms armature-dot-product is the engine's universal interpretation primitive and that social_contest exchange IS the sigma-A kernel (line 201). So pathology-as-churn here reuses both engine primitives, validating the 'the engine CAN do this' argument that grounds FG-4. Leverage stays high: this is a legitimate generalizable template. ONE correction to framing: the finding asserts §7.2 is 'the one place the system treats a non-decisive procedural outcome as GENERATIVE.' That is overstated — line 298 shows EVERY Compromise contest already generates a Priority-1 Scene Slate entry for the next season, and lines 305-311 add a Compromise-chain seeding/stalemate mechanic. Non-decisive outcomes seed churn corpus-wide via Scene Slate. What §7.2 uniquely does is MINT A NEW ACTOR / bifurcate the political map; it is not the only generative indecisive outcome. The strength survives because the actor-spawning property (the actual template to generalize) is genuinely unique to §7.2; only the rhetorical 'one place ... GENERATIVE' claim is inflated.
verdict.refined_improvement: Keep as a strength, but narrow the claim from 'the one place non-decisive outcomes are generative' to 'the one place a non-decisive outcome MINTS A NEW ACTOR' (the general Compromise→Scene-Slate path at §8.x/line 298 already makes indecisive outcomes churn-generative, just without spawning an entity). To generalize the template per the finding's intent without a bolt-on subsystem: when ANY Grand/Standard Contest ends in Compromise with sufficiently divergent Convictions between the parties, reuse the §7.2.1 split-rule and the armature Conviction-read to spawn a faction/sub-faction or schismatic actor, rather than only deferring tension to a Scene Slate entry. This reuses the existing sigma-A contest outcome, the armature dot-product identity-read, and the Key bus — no new resolver class.

strengths.title: Obligations + NPC-priority-tree binding is a faithful 'rule of recognition' output that seeds emergent arcs
strengths.research_basis: Hart's rule of recognition + the corpus thesis that the political act produces a BINDING that the institution remembers; robust action's payoff is that a win constrains opponents' future option-space, not just the present tally.
strengths.system_state: A decisive contest produces an Obligation tracked as a clock that, for NPCs, blocks any action violating it in the priority tree for its duration; Obligations even transfer across generational succession ('the institution remembers the commitment even if the individual doesn't').

locations.quote: the NPC priority tree is modified: any action that would violate the Obligation is blocked for the Obligation's duration unless the faction enters Survival priority (Stability ≤ 2). This means a won contest has lasting strategic impact — the player can constrain an NPC faction's behavior through political victory, not just stat changes.
Active Obligations transfer to new character's faction; the institution remembers the commitment even if the individual doesn't.

strengths.critique: This is the system at its best and the model for everything FG-1..FG-4 should imitate: the macro outcome (a constrained future) EMERGES from the granular per-exchange substrate and is validated top-down by a persistent clock that reshapes the AI's reachable action set. It is exactly the robust-action payoff — narrowing the opponent's option-space — rendered as a live game-state object, and the Survival-priority escape valve keeps it from being an inert cage. It maps cleanly onto the engine: clock bucket + priority-tree gate, no bolt-on.
strengths.churn_relevance: High-value churn done right: the Obligation's FAILURE MODE (an NPC violating it under Survival pressure, or a successor inheriting a commitment they didn't make) is itself a seeded arc. This is the template — the contest's OUTPUTS already generate emergent narrative; the gap is that its INPUTS (FG-1..4, who-sets-the-rules) do not yet.

verdict.evidence_quote: All NPC faction priority trees follow a standardised 7-level structure: | 1 | Survival | Universal | If Stability ≤ 2: take action that raises Stability or removes threat. Overrides all.
verdict.reasoning: Both cited quotes are verbatim-confirmed. §6.1 NPC-Obligation text is exact at social_contest_v30.md:286. The PC-death transfer line ("the institution remembers the commitment even if the individual doesn't") appears verbatim in TWO architecture docs: designs/architecture/generational_transition_v30.md:46 (§TRANSFER, with "New character inherits obligation countdown and violation conditions") and designs/audit/valoria_workplan_final.md:210. The mechanism the finding praises is genuinely load-bearing and engine-clean: (a) the NPC priority tree is a real standardised 7-level structure (npc_behavior_v30.md:772), and (b) Priority 1 = "Survival | If Stability ≤ 2 ... Overrides all" (npc_behavior_v30.md:776) is exactly the Survival-priority escape valve the finding names — so the Obligation gate ("blocked ... unless the faction enters Survival priority (Stability ≤ 2)") plugs into an existing override slot rather than inventing one. (c) Obligations are tracked as clocks per clock_registry_v30 (social_contest_v30.md:282), and the distillation report confirms clock_advance is one of the standing non-rolling primitive kinds and that "the contest IS the sigma kernel" (distillation report §1) — so the finding's "no bolt-on, clock bucket + priority-tree gate" claim is accurate. The strength is real as described and the leverage/effort framing (this is the template the other FGs should imitate) is well-calibrated.
verdict.refined_improvement: Original improvement is empty, consistent with a strength finding — keep it that way. One precise sharpening worth recording: the constraint is asymmetric. NPC Obligations are mechanically caged via the priority-tree gate, but Player Obligations are explicitly NOT (social_contest_v30.md:284: "The player is not forced to comply — they may violate it"). That asymmetry is correct design (player agency), but it means the celebrated "constrained future / narrowed option-space" payoff only literally exists for NPCs; for the player it is purely consequence-driven (Mandate/Reputation loss). If the template is to be generalised to FG-1..4, reuse the SAME two existing primitives — emit the Obligation as a clock-advance Key and let the consumer (priority tree for NPCs, consequence resolver for PCs) read it — rather than adding any new binding subsystem. Also note the GM-tractability cap of 3 active Obligations (ED-619, line 240) bounds how much emergent-arc churn this can actually seed at once.

strengths.title: Adjudicator-type-selects-primary-attribute cleanly maps Aristotle's three kinds of judge
strengths.research_basis: Aristotle (deliberation Part I.G) grounds the three oratory genera in three KINDS of judge — juror of the past, juror of the future, spectator who judges the speaker. The adjudicator is the firmest cross-domain correspondence (Part III.D / V.8): an impartial rule-applier who declares the outcome.
strengths.system_state: GM setup selects an adjudicator type, and that type determines which attribute doubles into the Argue pool: Expert Judge→Cognition, Crowd→Charisma, No-adjudicator→Attunement.

locations.quote: | Expert judge | A single authority evaluates arguments on merits | Royal Audience, Church Tribunal, Guild Arbitration | Cognition |
The primary attribute for the Argue roll shifts based on the adjudicator type.

strengths.critique: This is the system's strongest deliberative move: rather than one universal persuasion stat, the KIND of judge changes WHICH human faculty the contest tests. Expert Judge rewarding logical structure (Cognition), Crowd rewarding delivery/authority (Charisma), and No-adjudicator rewarding reading-the-counterpart (Attunement) is a near-direct instantiation of Aristotle's juror-of-the-past / spectator / future-oriented-deliberator triad. It keeps the contest honestly a deliberative game, not a flat opposed roll.
strengths.churn_relevance: Bottom-up-emergent compliant: the granular per-move pool is reshaped by the macro venue, so the same argument plays differently before a tribunal vs a crowd — which seeds different play and different stories per venue. This is the right altitude for the type-selection layer.

verdict.evidence_quote: the three social attributes (Cognition, Charisma, Attunement) each have a domain where they are primary. High-Cognition characters excel before judges and tribunals. High-Charisma characters excel before crowds and parliaments. High-Attunement characters excel in private dealings.
verdict.reasoning:
The mechanic is real and the cited quotes are verbatim. social_contest_v30.md §3 (lines 100-115) has the adjudicator-type → primary-attribute table (Expert Judge→Cognition, Crowd→Charisma, No adjudicator→Attunement); params/contest.md §Pools lines 19-22 confirms it as canonical (PP-234 redesign); and social_contest_v30_infill.md §3 line 26 states the design intent verbatim. So the strength itself is sound: the KIND of venue genuinely reshapes WHICH faculty the contest tests, and the churn claim (same argument plays differently per venue) holds.

What is overstated is the "near-direct instantiation of Aristotle's triad" framing and the leverage rating. Two of three legs map cleanly (Expert Judge ≈ forensic/juror-of-the-past; Crowd ≈ deliberative-assembly/spectator). The third leg is forced: the finding maps No-adjudicator→Attunement to Aristotle's "future-oriented deliberator / juror of the future," but the system's own §3 reasoning is "You must read the other party and calibrate" (line 108) — a private DYAD with NO third-party judge at all. Aristotle's whole point (per the research_basis) is that genus is defined by the KIND OF JUDGE; a no-adjudicator negotiation is the absence of a judge, not a third kind of one. It is also not temporally future-oriented in the spec. So the correspondence is 2-of-3, not "near-direct."

Leverage "high" with an empty improvement is inconsistent: a pure strength that proposes no change is not high-leverage, and the mechanic is explicitly UNVALIDATED — social_contest_v30.md line 611 / params line 168 SIM-DEBT-04: "Adjudicator-type pool variation untested. Charisma×2 and Attunement×2 pools need calibration." A high-leverage label on an uncalibrated, no-action strength overstates it. Corrected to low.

Note the Aristotle lineage is the critic's overlay — no Aristotle/forensic/epideictic/deliberative reference exists anywhere in designs/scene/ — which is acceptable for a correspondence-strength but means the "honestly a deliberative game" claim rests on the critic's reading, not on stated design rationale.
verdict.refined_improvement: Keep this as a preserved strength, not an action item. If anything is to be done, it is the existing SIM-DEBT-04 task, not a new subsystem: run sim/personal/contest.py to calibrate the Charisma×2 and Attunement×2 pools against the Cognition×2 baseline so the three venue-faculties are actually balanced (right now only the Cognition pool is implicitly tuned). Also tighten the finding's framing from "near-direct instantiation of Aristotle's triad" to "two-of-three correspondence": the No-adjudicator/Attunement case is the dyadic ABSENCE of a third-party judge, which is the inverse of Aristotle's judge-defines-genus principle, so present it as a deliberate divergence (private reading replaces an external judge) rather than the third leg of the triad. Resolve the still-open Panel/ED-137 case before claiming the type-selection layer is complete. No bolt-on subsystem is warranted; this already lives correctly on the sigma kernel.

strengths.title: Physical and substrate fouls ARE modeled as constitutive-rule violations with real penalties
strengths.research_basis: Pragma-dialectics (II.C): a fallacy is the violation of a discussion rule, a foul penalized within the game; the ten rules are constitutive.
strengths.system_state: Forfeits the violent party for chamber violence; gives the adjudicator a Certainty-indexed graded response to illicit Thread use (void / +1 Ob / note-only).

locations.quote: Violence in the contest chamber = immediate forfeit. Violent party auto-loses.
C5 (Orthodox) | Declares proceedings corrupted. Contest immediately suspended. Church Heresy Investigation fires

strengths.critique: Right shape: illegitimate move, detected by the institution, penalized, severity indexed to the adjudicator. The machinery for an argumentative-foul layer already exists and is proven.
strengths.churn_relevance: High-churn: a forfeit reframes the scene; a corrupted proceeding triggers a multi-season Heresy arc.

verdict.reasoning: Both cited quotes are verbatim and in-context. §9.6 (designs/scene/social_contest_v30_infill.md:49) reads exactly "Violence in the contest chamber = immediate forfeit. Violent party auto-loses." §9.4b (designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md:466) reads exactly the C5 (Orthodox) row: "Declares proceedings **corrupted**. Contest immediately suspended. Church Heresy Investigation fires...". The §9.4b table (lines 464-469) is precisely the "Certainty-indexed graded response" the finding describes: void (C5: results voided), +1 Ob (C4: "+1 Ob to all subsequent rolls by the Thread-using party"), note-only (C3: "Notes the event. No procedural consequence"). So the system-state description is accurate. The finding is a STRENGTH and is carefully scoped: it claims only that PHYSICAL and SUBSTRATE (Thread) fouls are modeled as constitutive-rule violations with real, institution-detected, severity-indexed penalties — it does NOT claim argumentative fallacies are covered. I independently confirmed the corpus has zero argumentative-foul vocabulary (no "fallacy/foul/ad hominem/straw man" in social_contest_v30.md, contest.md, or contest_extensions.md), so the finding is not overclaiming that layer; it explicitly says the "machinery for an argumentative-foul layer already exists and is proven" — i.e., the shape is present and extensible, which is true. Churn claim verified: §7.3 (lines 367-414) documents a real multi-phase Heresy Investigation lifecycle, and the §6/§7 sanction table (lines 248-251) confirms multi-season consequences (Tribunal "Until formally revoked"; Excommunication eligible). The forfeit/void genuinely reframes the scene. The only overstatement is the "high" leverage rating: this is an observational strength with an EMPTY improvement field and effort "low" — it requires no action and proposes nothing, so its actionable leverage is medium, not high. The verdict remains sound because the described strength is real and correctly located.
verdict.evidence_quote: | C3 (Questioning) | Notes the event. No procedural consequence. Adjudicator's internal conflict deepens — Conviction Scar check per §3.4.
verdict.refined_improvement: As a strength, no fix is needed. If extended into an argumentative-foul layer, do NOT add a bolt-on fallacy subsystem: reuse the existing §9.4b Certainty-indexed adjudicator response as the resolver template — route a detected illegitimate argumentative move (e.g., a move whose interpretation contradicts the speaker's own prior Threads/Beliefs) through the sigma kernel that already powers the contest, with the adjudicator's Conviction (via the armature dot-product against the move) setting severity, exactly as Certainty sets it for Thread fouls. The constitutive-rule shape is already proven by §9.6 + §9.4b; any future foul layer should clone it, not parallel it.

strengths.title: Obligations and Wager Obligations correctly model the post-deal binding as a future-conditional, ratifiable commitment
strengths.research_basis: Putnam two-level game: the durable output of a negotiation is a commitment that must survive ratification and future delivery; a deal is only as good as the parties' ability to bind themselves to future conditions. Mixed-motive bargains create surplus precisely by trading present concession against future-verifiable delivery.
strengths.system_state: A decisive win yields a binding Obligation — a specific, verifiable, time-bound commitment tracked as a clock with explicit violation consequences; Wager Obligations let the winner extend present trust against a named future condition.

locations.quote: The commitment must be achievable and verifiable (not "be good" but "withdraw Templars from Gransol within 2 seasons").
The losing side's commitment names a specific, time-bound, verifiable outcome in the future
any action that would violate the Obligation is blocked for the Obligation's duration unless the faction enters Survival priority

strengths.critique: This is the system at its strongest on this lens. The Obligation is genuinely a binding-commitment primitive: verifiable, clock-tracked, with violation consequences that bite the violator's faction stats — exactly the credible-commitment mechanism Putnam's framework needs to make a deal more than cheap talk. The Wager extends it to future-conditional delivery (present concession traded for future-verifiable outcome), which is integrative bargaining done right. The NPC-Obligation priority-tree block is especially well-mapped: a political win mechanically constrains an NPC faction's future moves, which is the durable payoff a bargain is supposed to produce.
strengths.churn_relevance: Strong churn alignment: the Obligation's FAILURE modes (violation → Mandate/Stability hit, +2 Ob on next DA, accelerated Heresy Investigation; Wager non-delivery → the deferred arc transition fires immediately) are themselves story seeds, not dead ends. The §6.1.1 edge cases (counterparty death, institutional collapse, structural impossibility) are exactly the generative failure-mode churn the axiom prizes.

verdict.reasoning: All three cited quotes are verbatim-present in designs/scene/social_contest_v30.md §6.1 (lines 244, 255, 286). The strength is real and the mapping to Putnam's credible-commitment / ratification framing is accurate. The Obligation is a genuine binding-commitment primitive: §6.1 requires it be "achievable and verifiable" (line 244), tracks it as a clock per clock_registry_v30 with source/parties/commitment/duration/violation-trigger (line 282), and attaches violation consequences that bite faction stats (Mandate/Stability, +2 Ob next DA — table line 249). The NPC-Obligation priority-tree block (line 286) does exactly what the finding claims: it mechanically blocks any NPC action that would violate the Obligation for its duration unless the faction drops into Survival priority (Stability ≤ 2) — a real future-constraint on faction behavior, not just a stat delta. The Wager extension (line 255) correctly models future-conditional delivery (present concession traded for a named, time-bound, verifiable future outcome). The churn claim also holds and is well-supported: failure modes are generative — §6.1.1 (ED-778, lines 270-276) specifies counterparty death (discharged neutrally + Renown +1), institutional collapse (suspended/successor-resume), and structural impossibility ("fails forward" → arc transition fires + Conviction Scar), and PC-death transfer is real: generational_transition_v30.md §46 confirms "Active Obligations: transfer to new character's faction ... New character inherits obligation countdown and violation conditions." So the deferred-arc / violation paths feed back into the engine rather than dead-ending. No corpus location contradicts the strength; if anything the system handles MORE than the finding states (settlement-targeted Obligations per settlement_bridge_unification C-08, line 253; full edge-case taxonomy). The improvement field is correctly empty for a confirmed strength. Note: Obligation is a spec/clock construct, not modeled in sim/personal/contest.py (no matches) — but the finding makes no sim claim, so this does not weaken it.
verdict.evidence_quote: **NPC Obligations:** When an NPC faction loses a contest and receives an Obligation, the NPC priority tree is modified: any action that would violate the Obligation is blocked for the Obligation's duration unless the faction enters Survival priority (Stability ≤ 2).
verdict.refined_improvement: None required — this is a confirmed strength with no gap to close. If any tightening is wanted: the fully future-conditional form (Wager Obligation) is gated to a narrow configuration (Grand Contest + Projection genre + Consequence Resonant Style, line 255), so the "integrative bargaining" generality the finding praises is only fully available in that path; base Obligations cover the verifiable-time-bound-commitment case generally. This is a deliberate scoping choice, not a defect — no new subsystem is warranted; the existing clock_registry + Key-bus violation consequences already carry the binding.

strengths.title: Bidirectional Persuasion Track + Chain Contests preserve the closeness/no-deal dynamics the research demands
strengths.research_basis: Mixed-motive bargaining and Priest-Klein both turn on CLOSENESS: the live, contestable middle is where the action is, and an unresolved-but-close dispute should persist and re-seed rather than collapse to a clean win. No-deal (BATNA) must remain a real, consequential option.
strengths.system_state: The track is bidirectional with a genuine compromise zone; Chain Contests re-open from the previous final track position (not reset to 5), and private negotiations with no tracker resolve a tie as a consequential stall rather than a forced winner.

locations.quote: The follow-up contest starts at the Persuasion Track's final position from the previous contest (not reset to 5).
the contest stalls: strain persists, all Read results from the contest become permanent knowledge ... The narrative moves forward — the failure to agree IS a consequential outcome.
the bidirectional persuasion track

strengths.critique: The bidirectional track + compromise zone is the right substrate for a distributive contest: it represents a continuous division of surplus rather than a binary flip, and the Chain Contest re-seeding correctly models a close-but-unresolved dispute persisting across seasons — the Priest-Klein insight that the closest disputes are the ones that recur. The private-negotiation stall is a genuine BATNA/no-deal outcome treated as consequential, not a failure to be papered over. These are exactly the primitives a ZOPA layer (FG-1) should be built ON TOP of, not replace — which is why the fixes above reuse the track rather than discarding it.
strengths.churn_relevance: Directly churn-positive and flagged load-bearing by the engine audit: the stall and the chain both convert non-resolution into forward narrative (permanent Read knowledge, a Scene Slate entry, a cold-equilibrium freeze). The system already treats 'no clean winner' as generative — which is the foundation the missing mixed-motive layer needs.

verdict.reasoning: All three cited quotes are verbatim-accurate and in-context. (1) §6.3 Chain Contests (line 301): "The follow-up contest starts at the Persuasion Track's final position from the previous contest (not reset to 5)." — confirmed, with supporting rules at lines 303-305 (Disposition/Scar carry-over) and the §6.3 chain terminator (max 3 contests -> cold equilibrium, Disposition freeze, line 311), which is exactly the "close-but-unresolved dispute persists and re-seeds" dynamic the finding claims. (2) §2 Step 4 (line 76) gives the private/no-adjudicator stall verbatim, reinforced by §6 (line 219) and params/contest.md line 137 ("Private tie (no tracker) | Stall; strain persists; Read info permanent; relationship stressed") and the §12 resolved-items table line 580 — so the BATNA/no-deal-as-consequential-outcome is genuine, multi-doc canon, not an artifact. (3) The engine audit's "Load-bearing, DO NOT cut" list names "the bidirectional persuasion track" twice (report lines 152 and 194). The compromise zone 4-6 (line 73, params line 100) is a true continuous middle band, not a binary flip. Crucially the finding does NOT overstate: it explicitly frames the missing mixed-motive/ZOPA layer as something to build ON TOP of this substrate, and the corpus independently corroborates that gap is open — §12 Future design work (line 603): "Negotiation compromise resolution (ZOPA-style) | Identified as structurally different from Persuasion Track, not designed." So the strength is real and the adjacent gap (FG-1) it supports is genuinely unfilled. This is a correctly-scoped strength entry.
verdict.evidence_quote: | Private tie (no tracker) | Stall; strain persists; Read info permanent; relationship stressed |
verdict.refined_improvement: Leave empty as-is (this is a strength entry). If any action is attached, it should be framed strictly as: the ZOPA/mixed-motive layer (FG-1) must REUSE the existing 0-10 bidirectional track and its 4-6 compromise zone as the surplus-division axis — i.e. parameterize a ZOPA sub-band within the existing track and route no-deal through the already-canonical private-stall outcome — rather than introducing a parallel negotiation subsystem. This keeps it on the sigma-resolver/Persuasion-Track primitive and respects the §12 note that ZOPA is "structurally different from Persuasion Track" by adapting the track, not bolting on a second resolver. Note that 'medium' leverage is contingent: the strength's value is realized only through the FG-1 gap it enables; standing alone it is descriptive.
